message.content:
They got ready for bed the way they always did now, with the small routines that had grown up around the illness.

Helen sat on the edge of her side of the mattress, massaging lotion into the thinner skin along her forearms. The lamplight picked out every ridge of tendon and the faint map of veins under her skin. She’d been proud of these arms once, strong from carrying children and groceries and stacks of library books. Now they looked like someone else’s—lighter, more fragile, the freckles scattered wider on a paler background.

She felt the mattress dip as David came back from the bathroom. She didn’t look up right away. She focused on rubbing the lotion into her knuckles, circling, circling, as if she could stay in that small task for the rest of the night and not move forward into whatever this was going to be.

“You want the light off?” he asked.

The question was simple, but it caught on the air between them. It wasn’t about the light. It hadn’t been for a long time.

Helen swallowed. “Maybe…leave it for a bit,” she said, surprising herself.

He nodded, even though her gaze was still on her hands. He sat on his side, facing her, his weight making tiny waves in the mattress that nudged at her hips. She could see him now in the corner of her vision: the soft swell of his stomach, the silver hair on his chest, the scar along his shoulder from the surgery fifteen years ago when they’d thought that was going to be their big medical scare.

They had been wrong.

David cleared his throat softly. “Um,” he said. “I warmed up the blanket, like you asked.” He gestured toward the folded throw at the foot of the bed. “So you don’t get chilled.”

She turned her hands palm up on her lap, fingers still glistening faintly with lotion. She finally met his eyes. They were as familiar as her own name—brown, edged now with a permanent fatigue, but open. Waiting.

“Thank you,” she said. “You remembered.”

He smiled, small and a little crooked. “I remember the important things.”

The unimportant things, she thought, were scattered all over the past two years: forgotten pharmacy pickups, missed TV shows, unpaid bills. But he had not forgotten the way the chill could creep into her bones at night since the treatments, how it could leave her teeth chattering. He hadn’t forgotten the particular pattern of her scar or how carefully the pajamas had to slide over tender skin.

He hadn’t forgotten her, even when she had tried to disappear.

She watched his hand move, slow and uncertain, to rest on the quilt between them. His fingers opened and closed once, as if testing the distance, testing his own courage. For months after the surgery, every touch had been medical or necessary: the nurse adjusting a tube, the aide helping her to the bathroom, his hand braced under her elbow as she shuffled down the hallway. Intimacy had been bartered for survival, and when survival finally, miraculously, seemed to have been purchased, they had no idea how to buy back the rest.

“May I…?” he began, and then stopped.

She shifted closer, the quilt rasping softly under her. Their knees brushed. It was such a small point of contact, bone against bone through thin cotton, but her breath caught anyway. She lifted her hand, still slightly damp, and laid it on top of his.

His skin was warm, his knuckles bumpy and familiar. He turned his palm up to meet hers. Their fingers threaded together automatically, the way they had done while waiting for lab results, in hospital elevators, in parking lots under bad fluorescent lights.

Now, though, the fluorescent glare was replaced by the lamplight’s warmer halo, his face softened by the shadows.

“You don’t have to,” he said quietly. “If it hurts. Or if you’re tired.”

She looked at their hands. The wedding band had grown loose on her finger; she’d almost lost it twice doing the dishes. His looked snug, the gold dimmed by time. They had not taken them off, not even when every other part of her had been stripped away and examined under harsh lights.

“I’m tired of being careful,” she said, and heard the tremor in her own voice. “But I’m more tired of being…afraid of you seeing me.”

His fingers pressed around hers, a gentle squeeze. “Hel,” he said. “I’ve seen you. All of you. Every version.”

“Not this one.” Her free hand crept up involuntarily to touch the edge of her shirt, where it met the scar that curved and dipped across her chest. Even through the fabric, she could feel the ridge
